metadata.title: One Mark. Every Move Remotion Knows.
metadata.description: A technique-showcase reel animating the Bear Brown initials mark every way Remotion can — 9:16 portrait edition. One wide mark (7500×5000 viewBox, complex path), twenty techniques, for review: which moves deserve to live?

beats.narration_text: Hey, this is Liam, in for Bear. One mark — Bear Brown's own initials. We're running it through every motion technique Remotion knows, back to back, nine by sixteen, so you can judge which ones deserve to live.
Spring Entrance. The mark scales up from zero with a spring config. Soft damping — does it feel weighted or just slow?
Overshoot Spring. Low damping, high stiffness — the mark overshoots past its landing and bounces back. Squash on impact, stretch on rebound. Physical, or just noisy?
Draw-On Stroke. evolvePath traces the path outline with a terracotta stroke, then the fill floods in. The complexity of this mark makes this one interesting — watch the corners.
Mask Reveal. First a left-to-right clip-path wipe, then a radial iris opens from center. Two masks, one beat — the iris is the more interesting of the two.
Scale Zoom. Linear ease in the first half, bezier in the second. The contrast is the point — one reads as machine, one reads as intention.
Rotation. Pivot entrance at negative forty-five, springs to upright, then a slow continuous turn. The mark is wide — rotation reveals it differently from every angle.
Skew And Shear. The mark leans in, holds the tension, then releases back to upright. Tension, or wobble — the hold is what decides it.
Opacity Through Blur. Fades in through a blur — arrives out of focus, sharpens on landing. Soft or cheap, depending on your tolerance for the effect.
Color Interpolation. Ink sweeps to terracotta and back — this is the one treatment beat, the palette violation is intentional. The brand's one accent color earns its own beat here.
Kinetic Grid. The mark tiled across the full nine-sixteen frame in a four-by-five grid, each cell staggered in with a ripple delay. Scales this wide mark down to postage-stamp size — does it hold?
Glitch Slices. Six horizontal slices each offset independently, then snap clean. The resolution should read as correction, not decoration.
Trail Echo. The mark slides across the stage with five ghost copies trailing behind, each fading. Motion lingers — or it just looks like a bad export.
Noise Wobble. Sine-based pseudo-noise wobbles the mark on three axes — X, Y, and rotation — with the amplitude decaying to stillness. Alive, or anxious?
Elastic Physics. Drops from above with a very low damping spring — squash on impact, elastic rebound. Spring config is everything — too stiff reads hard, too loose reads cheap.
Card Flip. Perspective rotateY, as if the mark is on a physical card flipping toward the camera, then a slow full rotation. The mark is wide — perspective distortion is aggressive here.
Shadow Play. A duplicate mark offset in X and Y acts as a shadow — it stretches ahead of the mark, then snaps back. Detached, then grounded.
Composer Summon. The Claude composer UI, a prompt typed in, the terracotta send spark fires — and the mark arrives. The interface is the origin.
Stroke Pulse. A stroke-dashoffset heartbeat rhythm — the outline of the mark pulses on and off at a steady tempo. Subtle, or too subtle?
Scale Breathe. A continuous oscillation between ninety-six and a hundred and four percent scale — one slow breath, looping. The quietest technique in the catalog.
Exit Family. Three exits back to back: shrink-spin, blur-out, mask-close iris. The word leaves three ways. Which one you would cut to black on — that is the judgment.
Now you have the receipts. Take this prompt, run it on your own SVG mark, and flag the two or three moves that actually serve your brand.
